explanation:
## Grass Seed Calculator for DIY Lawn Projects

Proper seed quantities are critical for successful lawn establishment and maintenance. This calculator uses industry-standard seeding rates based on grass type and lawn condition.

## How Seeding Rates Work

**New Lawns:** 30–50g/m² of quality grass seed
**Overseeding/Reseeding:** 20g/m² for filling sparse areas

These rates follow IRAM standards and international horticultural best practices. The variation accounts for different grass species, soil quality, and climate conditions.

## Using Your Results

Once you have the total seed amount needed, you can:
- Purchase the correct quantity to avoid waste
- Plan your seeding schedule
- Estimate project costs
- Prepare the soil accordingly

## Important Notes

These calculations are estimates based on standard industry rates. Actual seed requirements may vary by grass species, regional climate, soil condition, and seeding method. For specific recommendations for your lawn, consult a local landscaper or turf specialist. Data current as of 2026.

faq.q: How much grass seed do I need per square meter?
faq.a: For new lawns, use 30–50g/m². For overseeding existing lawns, 20g/m² is sufficient.

faq.q: What's the difference between new seeding and reseeding?
faq.a: New lawns need higher seed rates (30–50g/m²) to fill bare soil. Reseeding to fill gaps uses less (20g/m²).
Results are practical estimates with ±5–10% variation, depending on seed quality, soil condition, and climate.
Yes, all our calculators are completely free with no registration required.

faq.q: Do you save or track my data?
faq.a: No. Calculations run in your browser only. No data is sent to any server.

faq.q: What grass types does this work for?
faq.a: These rates apply to common cool-season and warm-season grasses. Consult local experts for specialty varieties.

faq.q: When is the best time to seed a lawn?
faq.a: Cool-season grasses: spring or fall. Warm-season grasses: late spring through early summer.

faq.q: Can I use metric rates for US lawns?
faq.a: Yes. These rates are universal. If you prefer sq. feet, convert: 1 m² ≈ 10.76 sq. ft.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology
